SlidingWindow: A reliable in-order channel still requires bookkeeping if the sender is allowed to pipeline up to W frames before the receiver acks. The five state variables fall out of that requirement directly: base names the oldest unacked frame, next names the slot the sender will use for its next transmission, expected names the next sequence number the receiver will accept, channel holds the in-flight frames as an ordered sequence, and delivered records what the receiver has actually consumed. The window invariant next - base <= W is enforced inline on SendFrame, which is the only action that can grow the gap. AdvanceBase only ever runs after expected has moved, so base trails expected, which itself trails next, giving the chain base <= expected <= next. The most subtle clause in TypeOK is the explicit prefix property delivered[i] = i - 1: it asserts that whatever the receiver has handed up is the literal prefix 0,1,2,..., which would fail instantly if DeliverHead ever consumed an out-of-order head. Boundedness comes from MaxSeq capping next plus the W cap on the in-flight gap, so the entire reachable space is small even though the protocol is logically unbounded.
Snapshot: The interesting modeling decision in Chandy-Lamport is that the marker is not a separate channel but a distinguished value (zero) interleaved with real data inside the same FIFO. That single trick lets a single sequence variable carry both ordinary messages and the snapshot signal in their true relative order, which is exactly the property the algorithm needs. recSender and recReceiver are sentinel-valued (NoRec) until each side has recorded, so a guard like recSender = NoRec on StartSnap ensures the initiator records exactly once. The recChannel variable is appended to only during the window between StartSnap firing and RecvMarker firing on the other side, capturing precisely the messages that crossed the cut. sentCount exists because data values themselves are content-free in this abstraction, so a monotonic counter is the only way to distinguish one data message from another. The TypeOK clauses recSender <= sLocal and recReceiver <= rLocal certify that the recorded local state is never ahead of the actual local state, which is the basic temporal soundness check that makes the recorded snapshot a valid past.
SpinLock: Three variables suffice because the test-and-set protocol distinguishes only three roles a process can occupy: not interested, spinning, or holding. The lock bit is technically redundant with the holder set, but keeping it separate lets the spec model the atomic CAS at the granularity the hardware actually offers, with TestAndSet conjoining lock = 0 (the test) and lock' = 1 (the set) in one action. The crucial guard sits at TestAndSet: only a process already in spinning may transition into holder, and the lock = 0 read forces serialization since at most one such transition can happen from a state where the lock is free. Once lock' becomes 1, every other spinning process is blocked at the same guard until Release writes 0. The SpinSafe conjunct holder \cap spinning = {} is what rules out the absurd intermediate where a process is both racing for and holding the lock; it is preserved because TestAndSet removes p from spinning in the very same step that adds it to holder, and Release does not touch spinning at all.
SplitBrain: The point of a witness in a two-replica system is that majority quorum is impossible with only two voters, so a third arbiter is consulted. The state captured here is minimal: writer names the unique active writer (or the sentinel NONE), and witnessSeen is the singleton set of replicas currently paired with the witness. Modeling witnessSeen as a set rather than a single value is what makes the safety argument crisp -- BecomeWriter conjoins both r \in witnessSeen and Cardinality(witnessSeen) = 1, so a replica can only become writer when it is the sole holder of the witness link. The SeeWitness action requires witnessSeen = {} as its precondition, which means the witness can never be simultaneously paired with two replicas, and that single pairing constraint is the lever the entire safety argument rests on. LoseWitness explicitly forbids dropping the witness link while still being the writer, which preserves the implication writer # NONE => writer \in witnessSeen across every step.
StopAndWait: Stop-and-wait is interesting precisely because its channel is not reliable -- it may deliver duplicates -- and yet the receiver must still hand each value upward exactly once and in order. The defense is the alternating bit, modeled here as sBit and rBit, plus the structural choice to represent the channel as a set rather than a sequence (a set naturally absorbs duplication, since adding an existing element changes nothing). RecvFresh fires only when the head bit equals rBit and then flips rBit, so a duplicated frame from the previous round arrives with the wrong bit and falls through to RecvDup, which re-acks but does not append to delivered. SenderAdvance is what makes the model finite under unbounded duplication: when the matching ack is observed, every old in-flight frame at the previous bit is purged from msgs. The strong safety lives in the TypeOK clause delivered[i] = i - 1, which asserts the literal prefix 0,1,...,i-1 -- a property that would be violated immediately if the bit-discrimination logic in RecvFresh were dropped.
StoreBufferTSO: What makes TSO worth modeling at all is that program order on a single core is preserved through the buffer even though the buffer hides values from the rest of the system. To make that observable in a state-based spec, every store is tagged with a fresh monotonically increasing nextSeq number; the actual data value would add nothing because the property is purely about ordering. The buffer sb is a sequence (FIFO) and the global memory log mem is also a sequence, so Drain literally moves Head(sb) to the tail of mem in one step. The two strong safety conjuncts MemInOrder and SbInOrder express the architectural contract -- both sequences are strictly increasing -- and the bridging clause mem[Len(mem)] < sb[1] forbids any seq number from appearing out of order across the buffer-memory boundary. Because seq numbers come from a single counter and only flow downstream, those three monotonicity facts are inductive: Issue extends sb at the tail with nextSeq, Drain pops at the head, and no action ever introduces an older number anywhere.
Sudoku4x4: Sudoku is unusual among the specs in this corpus because the only state variable is the grid itself, a function from cells to digits with 0 standing for blank. There is no controller, no message channel, no per-process pc -- the entire dynamics is the single Fill action that picks a blank cell and a digit and writes it. The strength of the specification lives in Fill's guards: NoDupAfter is invoked three times for the row, the column, and the containing 2x2 box, and each invocation forbids the chosen digit from already appearing among the other non-blank cells of that group. The Box helper does the integer-division arithmetic ((r-1)/2)+1 to map a cell to its box coordinates, which is the only nontrivial geometry in the spec. The standing invariant ValidGrid says the same three things hold globally, and it is inductive because Fill only fires when the new write would not create any of the three kinds of duplicate, so the global validity is preserved cell by cell. The initial puzzle is hand-coded as a near-complete legal grid with three blanks, ensuring the Init state itself satisfies the invariant.
SzymanskiMutex: Szymanski's algorithm uses a single integer per process taking values 0 through 4, and the entire protocol is a sequence of guarded transitions between those levels. Level 0 is non-critical; level 1 is the doorway; level 2 is the waiting room; level 3 is the inner doorway; level 4 is the critical section. The trick that delivers mutual exclusion plus bounded waiting is the way levels 1 and 2 split: PassDoor moves a level-1 process directly to level 3 if no one else has reached the inner room yet, but WaitInRoom diverts it to level 2 if some other process already has. EnterCS conjoins two predicates -- no other process is at level 4, and no smaller-id process is at level >= 3 -- and the second clause is the linear-waiting condition that breaks ties between two contenders at the doorway. The TypeOK conjunct (level[i] = 4 /\ i # j) => level[j] # 4 is exactly the mutual exclusion property; it is preserved because EnterCS already requires every other process to satisfy level[j] # 4 in its guard, and Leave only weakens.
TcpClose: The four-way termination is asymmetric: only the active closer transits through fin_wait, closing, and time_wait, while the passive side walks close_wait and last_ack. The spec captures this by giving aState and pState different state-set types (ActiveStates and PassiveStates) and using four boolean flags finA, finP, ackA, ackP to model individual segments in flight. The flags are flipped from true to false in the same step that the receiving side advances its state, so each segment is consumed exactly once and there is no need for a multiset of messages. The most informative invariant is (aState = "time_wait") => (pState \in {"last_ack", "closed"}): time_wait can only be entered after the active side has received the passive FIN, which means the passive side must have already moved past close_wait. Symmetrically (sState = "closed") => (pState \in {"last_ack", "closed"}) rules out the active closer terminating before the passive side has received its final ACK, which is the property that makes the protocol robust to lost final ACKs.
TcpHandshake: The three-way handshake is a tiny state machine, but its safety property is genuinely subtle: a peer's local belief that the connection is established must not be reachable until the matching message from the other side has been observed. The spec uses two state variables cState and sState (with disjoint allowable phases -- the client is never in listen, the server is never in syn_sent) and a single set-valued channel that holds at most one of {SYN, SYNACK, ACK} at a time. Each action both consumes the prior message from the channel and emits the next, so the conversation is forced into the right serial order. The implication (sState = "established") => (cState = "established") is the asymmetry that matters: the client establishes first by sending the final ACK, and only when the server consumes that ACK does it follow. Conversely (cState = "established") => (sState \in {"syn_recv", "established"}) blocks the client from establishing before the server has at least seen the SYN. Together those two implications rule out any unilateral transition into the established state.
TestAndSetMutex: This spinlock is the simplest hardware mutex it is possible to write down: a single boolean lock and a per-process pc taking three values. The atomicity of the test-and-set instruction is captured by collapsing the test (lock = FALSE) and the set (lock' = TRUE) into the single action TASsucceed, which is also the unique action that can move a process from tas to cs. TASfail is the spinning case -- it observes lock = TRUE, leaves lock alone, and keeps the process in tas so it will be retried. Because TASsucceed requires lock = FALSE, two processes cannot both move into cs from the same starting state; whichever fires first sets lock to TRUE and the other process is now stuck on TASfail until Release runs. The conjunct (pc[i] = "cs" /\ i # j) => pc[j] # "cs" in TypeOK is preserved straightforwardly: TASsucceed adds a process to cs only when no other process can be in cs (since cs membership requires lock = TRUE held by exactly the entrant), and Release removes a process from cs without affecting any other.
ThreePhaseCommit: Skeen's third phase exists to remove a coordinator-failure window in classical 2PC, and the spec models it by giving the coordinator four phases (init, ready, precommit, done) instead of two. Each participant has its own pState that walks working -> voted -> precommit -> commit, and the precommit step is what guarantees that if any participant has heard the precommit signal, the coordinator's decision must be commit (not abort). Collect gates the move to ready on every participant having voted, and PreCommit then gates the move to precommit on every vote being yes, so the phase chain enforces a global agreement before any participant can adopt a non-final state. DoCommit fires only when every participant has reached pState[p] = precommit, which means the coordinator only finalizes once it knows the decision is locked in everywhere. The SafetyInv conjuncts assert uniform agreement across decided participants and force every decided participant to match cDecision, ruling out any divergence between coordinator and participant outcomes.
TicTacToeLegality: Two state variables suffice because the game's legality rules depend only on the static cell values and whose move it is. The board is a function from the 3x3 cell grid to {X, O, _}, and turn alternates between X and O. PlayMark guards on board[c] = "_" so marks only land on empty squares, and on turn = m so the players strictly alternate. The interesting invariant is the count consistency clause: when it is X's turn, X and O must have placed the same number of marks, and when it is O's turn, X has placed exactly one more than O (since X moves first). That single arithmetic relation makes the global turn variable redundant with the multiset of marks on the board, which is the canonical sanity check for a turn-based game. The clause CountMark(b, X) + CountMark(b, O) + CountMark(b, _) = 9 is just the partition condition on the function's range, and Done is a stutter step that fires when the board is full so the model has somewhere to go after the last legal move.
TicketLifecycle: A workflow with four nominal states (open, in_progress, resolved, closed) plus a reopen edge from closed back to open could in principle be modeled with a single status variable, but doing so would lose the historical fact that a closed ticket once passed through in_progress. The spec adds two ghost booleans ever_in_progress and ever_resolved that latch true on the corresponding transitions and never reset, so the safety property (status = "closed") => (ever_in_progress /\ ever_resolved) can be stated as a simple state predicate rather than a temporal one. The reopens counter is bounded by MaxReopens and gates the Reopen action, which keeps the otherwise-cyclic state graph finite. The history flags survive across Reopen because Reopen does not touch them, which is exactly what makes the invariant inductive: once true they stay true, and the only way to enter resolved or closed is via Resolve and Close, both of which respect the chain.
TicketLock: A ticket lock turns mutex acquisition into queue service: each process draws a monotonically increasing ticket number, and the holder of the ticket equal to now_serving + 1 is permitted to enter. The state has three variables: next_ticket (the dispenser), now_serving (the call number), and a per-process ticket function (with 0 meaning untaken). TakeTicket atomically reads next_ticket and writes both next_ticket' = next_ticket + 1 and ticket'[p] = next_ticket + 1, so two processes that race for a ticket get distinct values. Release advances now_serving by exactly one and clears the holder's slot, which by construction unblocks at most one waiting process. The TicketSafe conjunct Cardinality(Holders) <= 1 follows from the uniqueness of the ticket-vs-now_serving relationship: Holders is defined as the set of processes whose ticket equals now_serving + 1, and since tickets are pairwise distinct, that set has at most one element. The third TicketSafe clause asserts that distinct processes have distinct tickets when both nonzero, which is preserved by the atomic increment.
TlbShootdown: A TLB shootdown is fundamentally a barrier: the initiator may not declare the operation complete until every core has acknowledged. The spec uses four variables to make that contract observable: tlbValid is a per-core boolean indicating whether the cache line is still cached, requested is the in-progress flag set by Initiate, acked is the set of cores that have responded, and completed is the latch the initiator flips when its barrier closes. Complete conjoins acked = Cores so the latch can only fire after every core has been heard from. The strong invariant CompletedImpliesAllInvalid is the architectural guarantee: a completed shootdown means no TLB anywhere still holds the entry. Its inductiveness rests on AckedImpliesInvalid plus the fact that InvalidateAndAck is the only action that adds to acked and it does so in the same step that flips tlbValid[c] to FALSE; combining those two invariants, when acked = Cores every TLB is invalid, and that is exactly when Complete is allowed to fire.
TokenRing: On a token ring there is exactly one token in circulation, modeled here as a single integer holder pointing to whichever station currently has it; that representation makes the existence and uniqueness of the token a structural property of the type rather than a fact requiring a separate invariant. Each station has a mode of either idle or transmitting, and StartTx is guarded by holder = s so only the token-holding station can begin sending. PassToken is guarded by mode[s] = "idle", which prevents a station from passing the token mid-transmission. The two TypeOK implications -- transmitting implies being the holder, and two transmitting stations must be the same station -- are both inductive: the only action that introduces a transmitting station is StartTx which requires holder = s, and PassToken cannot run while any station is transmitting because it requires the holder itself to be idle, so transmission and ownership stay welded together at every step.
TournamentMutex: The tournament construction reduces N-process mutual exclusion to a binary tree of 2-process Peterson nodes, but the k = 1 instance modeled here is just the root node -- two processes contending at a single Peterson cell. The (flag, turn) pair is the standard Peterson signature: flag[i] announces interest, and turn is set by the entering process to its opponent ("after you"). The win condition at WinRoot is the disjunction flag[Other(i)] = FALSE \/ turn = i, which says either the other process is not competing or it has politely yielded the turn. Mutual exclusion comes from the fact that if both processes are simultaneously at wait_root, then both have set their flag to true and one of them must have written turn most recently, so exactly one of them satisfies turn = i. The conjunct (pc[i] = "cs" /\ i # j) => pc[j] # "cs" is preserved because WinRoot is the only entry into cs and its guard fails for the loser of the turn race until Release clears the winner's flag.
TowersOfHanoi: The natural representation of a Hanoi configuration is one sequence per peg, listed bottom to top, so the top disk is at index Len(s) and a legal move is a top-to-top transfer. The disk-size constraint -- a larger disk may never sit on a smaller one -- collapses to the property that each peg's stack is strictly decreasing from bottom to top. Move's guard captures the legal condition: either the destination peg is empty, or its current top is strictly larger than the source's top. The strong invariant SafetyInv is the conjunction of three Decreasing predicates plus the disk-conservation clause Len(pegs[A]) + Len(pegs[B]) + Len(pegs[C]) = N. Both halves are inductive: Move only ever pops from the source's top and pushes a strictly smaller (or the only) value onto the destination, so neither stack can become non-decreasing in a single step; and conservation is trivial because Move is a swap (one disk leaves source, the same disk arrives at destination).
TransferAtomic: The atomicity claim of bank transfer is best demonstrated by making debit and credit a single state transition. The Commit action writes both balance[src] - 1 and balance[dst] + 1 in one update via the EXCEPT-with-two-positions form, so no intermediate state ever exists where money has left the source but not yet arrived at the destination. Conservation of total balance is the canonical safety property, and it is asserted in TypeOK via a recursive SumOver helper that walks the Accounts set. SumOver(Accounts) = Total is inductive because Commit's net effect on the sum is -1 + 1 = 0 and Abort touches no balances at all, while the initial state assigns MaxAmount to every account by construction. The guard balance[src] >= 1 prevents overdraft, which together with the Account-typed range 0..Total in TypeOK rules out negative balances. txStatus is a separate per-transaction variable so the spec can model concurrent in-flight transactions without any single transaction being repeated.
TricolorGc: Tricolor marking encodes reachability by coloring each object white, gray, or black, where the discipline is that gray objects are known reachable but their children have not yet been examined. The single state variable color is a function from objects to those three colors, and the static reference graph is a chain encoded by the helper Edges(i) = {i - 1}. Scan(o) is the heart of the algorithm: it transitions o from gray to black, and in the same step it pre-grays any white child of o so that the child becomes part of the gray work set. Pre-graying before turning the parent black is exactly what preserves the tricolor invariant -- no black object points at a white object -- which is the well-known soundness condition for incremental and concurrent garbage collectors. ReachableMarkedAtFixpoint is the secondary invariant that says once the gray set is empty, no reachable object has been left white; in this chain that is equivalent to saying the entire chain has been marked, since every object is reachable from the root.
Trie: The trie is the most aggressively abstracted spec in the slice: the actual tree structure is gone, and all that remains is the set of inserted words drawn from a hand-enumerated 4-element domain (length-2 strings over {a, b}). The reasoning is that for the safety property -- lookup returns true exactly when insert was previously called -- the trie's internal layout is irrelevant; only set membership matters. Insert is guarded to forbid duplicate insertion and to bound the cardinality at K, and Lookup is a no-op observation conditioned on membership. The Bounded invariant is therefore both the type constraint and the strong safety claim: inserted is a subset of the static Words set with cardinality at most K. The spec's brevity is the point -- it documents that for the given correctness contract, the implementation freedom is total, and the model checker need only verify that no action ever causes a lookup to succeed for an uninserted word, which holds trivially since Lookup's guard is exactly w \in inserted.
TwoFactorAuth: Two-factor auth is a linear pipeline with branching failure: from none the user proceeds to factor1_ok or denied, and from factor1_ok proceeds to factor2_ok or denied, and only from factor2_ok can GrantSession transition to granted. To make the safety property granted => both factors verified provable as a state predicate, the spec introduces ever_factor1 and ever_factor2 as monotone history flags that latch true on the corresponding verify actions. The implication chain in SafetyInvariant is what links the present-tense phase to the historical record: granted requires both flags true, and the secondary clause factor2_ok => ever_factor1 prevents skipping factor 1 even from the intermediate phase. Failure transitions go directly to denied without touching the flags, so a denied session never accidentally satisfies the granted precondition. Done is a stutter on the terminal phases so the model can keep generating successor states without contributing to the reachable set.
TwoPhaseCommit: Classical 2PC has the coordinator in one of two phases (init or decided) and each participant in one of four (working, voted, commit, abort). The coordinator's decision is taken atomically: DecideCommit fires only when every vote is yes, and DecideAbort fires when at least one vote is no. The participants then transition into their final state via Learn, which copies cDecision into pState[p]. Because Learn is the only action that can move a participant into commit or abort, every decided participant's value is by construction equal to whatever cDecision held at the moment Learn fired; and since cDecision is set exactly once per round (by DecideCommit or DecideAbort, never both) the uniform-agreement property follows. SafetyInv writes that out as two conjuncts: any two decided participants agree, and any decided participant matches cDecision. Reset rolls the world back to working at the end of a round so the model checker can explore further rounds without the state space exploding.
TwoPhaseLockingDeadlock: Strict 2PL on two resources is the textbook example of a deadlock waiting to happen: if transaction t1 grabs R1 then waits for R2, and t2 grabs R2 then waits for R1, the waits-for graph has a cycle and neither transaction can make progress. The spec models the lock table as holds : Resources -> Txns \cup {NONE} and the waits-for graph as a per-transaction wait pointer waits : Txns -> Txns \cup {NONE}. Acquire is allowed only when the requesting transaction is not currently waiting on anything, which keeps each transaction's state classifiable; Wait sets waits[t] := holds[r] when the lock is taken by another, snapshotting the dependency. Release atomically clears every lock the transaction holds and zeroes out every waits pointer that referenced it, modeling commit/abort as a single atomic teardown. The TypeOK clause waits[t] # NONE => \E r : holds[r] = waits[t] enforces that the dependency graph never contains stale edges -- a waiting transaction is always blocked on an actual current holder.
TwoProcessHandshake: A two-process mutex with asymmetric tie-breaking is interesting because the asymmetry allows safety to hold without a turn variable. Each process has its own request flag (req_a, req_b) and a per-process pc taking values ncs, wait, cs. Process A advances from wait to cs only when req_b = FALSE -- it strictly defers to B -- and additionally A's BackoffA action lowers req_a entirely if it sees B has raised its request, ceding priority. Process B's EnterB has the symmetric guard pc[a] # cs and does not back off, which is what gives B priority on simultaneous requests. The result is that the only way both processes can be in cs simultaneously would require A to enter while req_b is false and B to enter while pc[a] is not cs, but A entering sets pc[a] to cs which falsifies B's guard, so the race is closed. The conjunct pc[p] = cs => pc[q] # cs in TypeOK is preserved by exactly that case analysis on which process moves first.
UnboundedChannel: When the only safety property of interest is the conservation relation between sent and received messages, there is no need to track individual message contents -- two counters are sufficient. sent is the cumulative number of messages produced and received is the cumulative number consumed, and the invariant pending = sent - received with pending bounded above by Max captures the channel's contract precisely. Recv is guarded by received < sent, which is the natural blocking condition for an empty channel. The Drain action exists only to keep the model finite: when both counters reach Max it resets them to zero so further behaviour can be explored, which is a standard trick for modeling unbounded systems with bounded explorations. ChannelInvariant has three conjuncts -- received <= sent, the difference is non-negative, and the difference is at most Max -- and they are all preserved trivially by Send (which only increments sent, growing the difference up to its Max bound) and Recv (which only increments received, shrinking the difference but never below zero because of its guard).
UnionFind: The point of modeling union-find at the spec level is to verify the partition property after each union, not to study the asymptotic complexity of path compression. The single state variable parent maps each universe element to its representative, and the spec assumes a flattened representation in which parent[parent[x]] = parent[x] -- every element points directly at its root. Find is therefore a one-step lookup, and Union(a, b) atomically rewrites parent so that every element previously pointing at ra now points at rb, conditioned on the two roots actually being distinct. The Partition invariant has three conjuncts: parent values stay within the universe, every parent pointer is idempotent (parent[parent[x]] = parent[x]), and every parent[x] is itself a representative. Idempotence is preserved because Union rewrites all old-rep entries in lockstep: any element that pointed at ra now points at rb, and rb was already a fixed point, so the new parent's parent is still itself.
VectorClock: Vector clocks encode happens-before by giving each process a vector of counters, one entry per process, with the local entry growing on every event and the merge rule taking the elementwise max on receive. The spec models two processes and represents clock as a function Procs -> (Procs -> 0..MaxTick). LocalEvent simply increments clock[p][p]. Send packages a copy of the local vector (with the local entry already ticked) into a channel message tagged with the sender. Receive computes merged as the elementwise Max of the local vector and the message's vector, then ticks the receiver's own entry by one more. The pivotal safety conjunct is clock[p][q] <= clock[q][q]: the entry for q in any vector anywhere in the system never exceeds q's own current local time. That property is inductive because the only way p's view of q can grow is via Receive, which copies entries from a message that itself was constrained to satisfy the same bound when it was sent, plus q itself only grows clock[q][q] monotonically.
VersionedKv: A versioned KV store with last-write-wins semantics needs only two parallel arrays: versions[k] is the highest version ever assigned for key k, and store[k] is the value (which the spec equates to the version number itself, since the actual payload is irrelevant for the safety property). Put atomically increments versions[k] and writes the new version into store[k] in the same step, so the standing relation store[k] = versions[k] is preserved across every action. Get is a no-op because reads are non-mutating and the safety property is purely about state invariants, not return values. The Valid conjunct in TypeOK is exactly that equality, plus the type-bounded range versions[k] \in 0..MaxVersion, and it is inductive trivially: the only writing action is Put, and Put writes the same value to both arrays.
ViewChange: A PBFT-style view change is captured here as a quorum-driven primary installation: replicas accumulate vote messages for a target view, and once a majority has been collected, the deterministic primary for that view takes over. The state has three variables -- vcMsgs (per-view set of voters), currentView (per-replica), and primary (per-view, with 0 meaning not yet installed). Install is guarded by both vcMsgs[v] \in Quorum and primary[v] = 0, so a view can only install once and only with a majority backing. The deterministic primary function PrimaryOf(v) = ((v - 1) % N) + 1 means there is no choice involved at install time -- the safety conjunct primary[v] # 0 => primary[v] = PrimaryOf(v) is automatic by construction, but stating it as an explicit invariant is what makes the at-most-one-primary-per-view property checkable. ProposeView is guarded by v > currentView[r] so a replica cannot propose a view it has already moved past.
VotingMajority: Majority voting in a small finite electorate is captured by two state variables: ballot mapping each voter to their chosen candidate (or none), and winner storing the declared candidate (or none). Vote is guarded to fire only while winner = none, so once a winner is declared no further votes are accepted in that round. Tally is a derived expression -- the cardinality of the set of voters who chose c -- and Declare is guarded by Tally(c) > Half, where Half is the floor of N/2 (here 1 for N = 3), so the strict-majority condition is enforced inline. The safety invariant winner # none => Tally(winner) > Half is then inductive because the only action that sets winner is Declare, which establishes the strict-majority precondition, and no later action can lower a candidate's tally without also clearing the winner (Reset does both atomically). At-most-one-winner follows from the singleton type of winner.
WaitGroup: A Go WaitGroup is essentially a counted barrier: Add increments a counter, Done decrements, and Wait blocks until the counter reaches zero. The two-variable encoding here is the minimum that captures the externally observable contract: counter is the outstanding-task count and released is the latch flipped by Wait when it observes counter = 0. Add is guarded by ~released, which models the documented restriction that you may not call Add after Wait has returned. Done is guarded by counter > 0 so the counter cannot go negative. WaitReturn is the only action that can flip released, and its guard counter = 0 is what makes the safety conjunct released => counter = 0 inductive: once released is true, the only action that can change counter is Reset (which resets both atomically), and Reset is itself guarded by counter = 0. Boundedness comes from the MaxCount cap on Add.
WaterJugs: The Die Hard puzzle has only two state variables -- the water level in each of two jugs of fixed capacity 5 and 3 -- and six actions: fill, empty, pour for each jug. The interesting action is the pour, which transfers Min(source, destinationCapacity - destinationLevel) liters in one step, capturing the physical constraint that pouring stops when the source empties or the destination fills. The safety property is purely the type constraint big \in 0..BigCap and small \in 0..SmallCap, which sounds trivial but is exactly what would fail if Pour's amount calculation were wrong (an unconstrained transfer could leave a jug overfull or with negative volume). Because amount is computed as a min of two non-negative quantities and is then both added to one jug and subtracted from the other in the same step, the bounds are automatically preserved. There is no goal predicate in Next -- the spec simply enumerates the legal moves and lets the model checker verify that no reachable configuration violates the capacity constraints.
WitnessReplication: Primary-backup with a witness is the cheap-quorum trick: a third replica holds no data but votes to break ties, so a write is committed once the primary plus either the backup or the witness has acknowledged it. The spec compresses each replica's log to a single integer length, since the safety property concerns prefix containment rather than per-entry contents. Append grows log[primary]; Replicate grows log[backup] toward the primary; WitnessVote grows log[witness] toward the primary; and Commit advances committed by one once a majority of replicas hold that length. The strong invariant committed > 0 => |{r : log[r] >= committed}| >= 2 is inductive because Commit is the only action that grows committed and it requires the majority condition in its guard, while no other action ever shrinks any log[r] -- the durability of replicated entries is monotonic. The witness participates in the cardinality count exactly the same way as a real replica, which is the whole point of the construction.
WolfGoatCabbage: The natural state for the river-crossing puzzle is two sets, left and right, partitioning the four items, plus a single boat-position variable. Modeling banks as sets rather than per-item booleans makes the forbidden-pair condition clean: SafeBank(b) is true if either the farmer is present, or neither {wolf, goat} nor {goat, cabbage} is a subset of b. CrossLR uses an existential over the carry set -- either just the farmer alone or the farmer plus one item from the source bank -- and conjoins SafeBank checks on the post-state of both banks, so an unsafe move is simply not enabled. There is no separate validation pass; the legality predicate is a precondition of every action. SafetyInv is the static restatement of the same predicate, and it is inductive because no action fires unless its post-state already satisfies it. Boundedness is structural: with four items distributed across two banks plus a binary boat position, the reachable graph has at most a few dozen nodes.
WorkPool: A worker pool of fixed capacity W is captured by partitioning the task universe into three disjoint sets queued, inflight, and done. Pull is guarded by Cardinality(inflight) < W so the pool cannot oversubscribe, and Finish moves a task from inflight to done. The Recycle action sends a finished task back to the queue, which is purely a model-checking convenience to keep the state graph from terminating prematurely. The strong invariant PoolInv has five conjuncts: the cardinality cap on inflight, three pairwise disjointness statements, and the conservation equation queued \cup inflight \cup done = Tasks. All five are inductive because each action moves exactly one task between exactly two of the sets and never adds or removes task identities -- Pull moves from queued to inflight, Finish from inflight to done, Recycle from done to queued -- so the partition is preserved and the cap is enforced inline at Pull.
WorkStealing: Two workers each hold a local task count and a completed count, and the work-stealing rule is that an idle worker may take one task from the other's tail when the other has more than one. The four counters localA, localB, doneA, doneB are sufficient because the spec is interested only in the conservation property, not in task identities or scheduling order. ProcessA decrements localA and increments doneA atomically, which is the head-of-deque pop. StealAfromB is guarded by localB = 0 (B is idle) and localA > 1 (A has at least two so the steal does not race with A's own next pop), and it transfers exactly one task. Restart is the model-checker convenience that recycles all tasks back to A's deque once everything is done. The Conservation invariant localA + localB + doneA + doneB = InitTasks is preserved by every action because every action changes two counters by +1 and -1 (or, in the case of Restart, sets the sum back to InitTasks explicitly).
WorkflowEngine: The generic three-phase workflow has only a forward chain pending -> active -> done, and the safety claim is that no transition is ever skipped. Three variables capture this: the current state and two latching booleans ever_active and ever_done that record whether the corresponding phase has ever been entered. Activate transitions pending to active and sets ever_active; Complete transitions active to done and sets ever_done. The safety conjuncts encode the implications phase by phase: pending implies neither flag is set, active implies ever_active, and done implies both flags. Inductiveness rests on the fact that the latches are only ever raised in the actions that perform the corresponding state transition, and the actions themselves are guarded by the predecessor state, so it is impossible to arrive at done without first having taken Activate. Stay is a stutter on the terminal state so the model never deadlocks.
WriteAheadLog: The defining property of a WAL is durable-before-applied: a record is appended to the persistent log first, and only then is it allowed to take effect on the data state. The spec uses two state variables -- log as a sequence and applied as a counter -- and exactly two actions. LogWrite extends the sequence at the tail with the next natural number, and LogApply increments applied without touching log. The guard applied < Len(log) on LogApply is the load-bearing one: it forces strict prefix application, since the next applied value is always exactly the next undurable record. The TypeOK conjunct \A i \in 1..Len(log) : log[i] = i is the canonical-content invariant -- the log is always the literal sequence 1,2,3,... which is preserved trivially because LogWrite always appends Len(log) + 1. Together applied <= Len(log) and the canonical-content clause make the recovered data state observably equal to the log prefix of length applied.
WriteBuffer: A bounded per-core write buffer is a FIFO of pending stores plus a visible-memory abstraction. The spec has four state variables: buffer (the FIFO), memory (the visible architectural state), retired (a counter of stores already committed), and issued (a bound counter to keep the model finite). Issue appends a value to the tail of buffer if there is capacity, Commit pops the head of buffer and adds its value to both memory and retired in the same step. The most important safety conjunct is the simple equation memory = retired, which is inductive precisely because Commit updates them with the same delta (Head(buffer)) atomically -- if Commit ever updated only one of them or used different deltas, the equation would break. Bounded enforces Len(buffer) \in 0..K, which Issue's guard preserves directly. The MaxIssue cap exists only to bound the reachable space; the architectural contract is the equality between memory and the running sum of retired stores.
WriteThroughCache: Write-through means every write updates both the cache and main memory in the same step, so the cache is never allowed to drift away from memory while it is valid. Three variables suffice: mem holds main memory's value, cached holds the cache line's value, and valid is a boolean indicating whether the cache currently holds the line at all. Write is the load-bearing action: it sets mem' = v and cached' = v in one step and unconditionally validates the cache, which is what makes the coherence invariant hold across every write regardless of prior cache state. LoadFromMemory copies mem into cached when the cache is invalid and validates it. ReadHit is a stutter. Evict drops valid to FALSE without touching either value, which is safe because the invariant CacheCoherent only constrains cached when valid is true. The implication valid => cached = mem is inductive: Write establishes equality fresh; LoadFromMemory establishes it by copying; ReadHit and Evict cannot break it.
